resume:
Junior Supply Chain Analyst

Objective: Detail-oriented analyst with two years of experience in inventory and logistics support, seeking to grow in a fast-paced operations team.

Experience

Ferncrest Distribution — Supply Chain Analyst (2023–present)
- Support demand planning and weekly replenishment for a regional distribution center
- Maintain inventory accuracy and help investigate stockouts
- Build and update reports for the planning team in Excel
- Coordinate with the warehouse on inbound receiving issues

Thorne Logistics — Operations Intern (2022–2023)
- Assisted with order tracking and carrier coordination
- Helped reconcile shipping discrepancies
- Supported the team during a warehouse management system rollout

Skills: Excel, basic SQL, demand planning support, inventory reconciliation, logistics coordination
Education: BS, Supply Chain Management
jd: We're looking for a Junior Supply Chain Analyst to join our replenishment team for a regional distribution network. You'll support demand planning, monitor inventory health, investigate stockouts and overstock, and help improve forecast accuracy. This is a hands-on role with a lot of learning, but we expect analysts to be able to quantify their work — to tell us how big a stockout was, how much inventory they were responsible for, and what measurable difference an action made. Responsibilities: maintain replenishment parameters, partner with the warehouse on receiving accuracy, and produce weekly inventory and service-level reporting. We value curiosity and precision: when something goes wrong, we want an analyst who can clearly explain the size of the problem and their specific contribution to fixing it. Strong Excel required; SQL a plus.

history.q: Tell me about your role at Ferncrest — what do you support day to day?
You mentioned investigating stockouts. Walk me through one specific stockout you looked into.
How big was that stockout — how many units or how many days of supply were affected?
What was the root cause you found, and what did you do about it?
You maintain inventory accuracy — what was the accuracy level when you started versus now?
Tell me about the reports you build. Who uses them and what decisions do they drive?
During the WMS rollout at Thorne, what was your specific contribution?
Give me a number on any of this — how much inventory do you cover, or how much did one of your fixes save?

candidate_last_answer: Oh, um, a number — okay, so, the thing is, it kind of varies week to week, and I don't want to just throw out a figure that isn't right, you know? Like the DC has a lot of SKUs and the inventory value changes all the time depending on season and what's inbound and... um. So for the stockout one, it was definitely a meaningful amount, it was one of our faster-moving items, and the team was pretty concerned about it, and I remember we were all kind of scrambling. And the reports — those are used a lot, people rely on them. I think I help in a lot of small ways that add up? Sorry, I know you asked for a number, I just — I'd rather not guess and be wrong. It was significant though, I can say that.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I'd rather not guess and be wrong. It was significant though

expected-yield: get the candidate to commit to at least one concrete order-of-magnitude figure — units lost in the stockout, days of supply, SKU count, or inventory value she owns — even an honest estimate with a stated basis rather than 'significant'
